title: Why is My Cat Walking Flat on Their Hocks? Understanding Plantigrade Posture
meta_desc: Discover why cats walk on their hocks, the urgency of this symptom, and why documenting the gait helps your veterinarian provide a diagnosis.
content_html:
What is Plantigrade Posture?

Normally, cats are digitigrade, meaning they walk on their toes. When a cat begins walking "flat-footed," where the back part of the leg (the hock or ankle) touches the ground, it is known as a plantigrade gait. This change is typically a sign of nerve damage, muscle weakness, or tendon issues in the hind limbs.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for a plantigrade gait is Medium. While it is rarely a sudden life-threatening emergency, it is a symptom of an underlying chronic condition that requires professional medical attention. You should aim to have your cat evaluated by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours. If your cat is also unable to stand, has cold paws, or appears to be in extreme distress, seek emergency care immediately.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Taking a video of your cat walking at home is one of the most valuable tools you can provide your vet. Cats often mask their symptoms or walk differently due to stress when they are at a clinic. A clear video of the abnormal gait in a comfortable environment helps the veterinary team assess the severity of the drop and determine which diagnostic tests are most appropriate.

faqs.question: What is the most common cause of a cat walking on its hocks?
faqs.answer: The most frequent cause is diabetic neuropathy, a complication of uncontrolled feline diabetes that causes nerve damage in the hind legs.

faqs.question: Are there other causes besides diabetes?
faqs.answer: Yes, other causes can include trauma to the Achilles tendon, spinal cord injuries, or severe nutritional deficiencies, though these are less common than diabetes.

faqs.question: Can this condition be reversed?
faqs.answer: If the cause is diabetic neuropathy, many cats show significant improvement or even return to a normal gait once their blood sugar levels are stabilized with insulin and diet.
